question_title: Will any state actor use chemical weapons in Ukraine before May 1, 2026?
summary: The fine print restricts "chemical weapon" to OPCW Schedule 1 chemicals only (nerve agents like sarin/VX/Novichok, mustard agents, lewisites, ricin, saxitoxin). Despite 4 years of war and 12,000+ documented chemical incidents in Ukraine, ALL confirmed use involves CS gas (not scheduled) and chloropicrin (Schedule 3) — none are Schedule 1 chemicals. The EU's May 2025 sanctions, OPCW Technical Assistance Visit reports, and NATO accusations all reference CS gas specifically. Using Schedule 1 chemicals on the battlefield would represent a massive escalation threshold that Russia has not crossed despite extensive lower-level chemical use. Russia possesses and has used Novichok for targeted assassinations abroad, but battlefield deployment is a fundamentally different context with far greater deterrence. The ~2-month window further constrains probability.

factors.description: No precedent of Schedule 1 chemical use in Ukraine: Despite 4 years of war and 12,000+ documented chemical incidents, ALL confirmed use involves CS gas (riot control agent) and chloropicrin (Schedule 3). No nerve agents, mustard agents, or other Schedule 1 chemicals have been credibly alleged or confirmed in Ukraine.

Massive escalation threshold: Using Schedule 1 chemicals (nerve agents) would be qualitatively different from CS gas use, potentially triggering NATO intervention. Russia has maintained restraint at this threshold despite extensive lower-level chemical use.
Short time window (~2 months): Only Feb 22 to May 1, 2026 for Schedule 1 use to occur, be detected, and be credibly accused.

Verification and accusation lag: OPCW investigations take months. A NATO accusation could come faster but still requires evidence. Even if use occurred, credible accusation within the window is uncertain.

Ongoing chemical escalation pattern with 12,000+ incidents and Western warnings about possible nerve agent deployment. Russia is already pushing boundaries. Non-zero probability of crossing Schedule 1 threshold.
Russia has demonstrated capability and willingness to use Novichok (Skripal 2018, Navalny 2020), proving Schedule 1 agent possession. Battlefield context is different but capability exists.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 3% probability (logit: -3.5)**

The key to this question is the fine print, which restricts "chemical weapon" to **OPCW Schedule 1 chemicals only** — nerve agents (sarin, VX, Novichok), mustard agents, lewisites, ricin, and saxitoxin. This is far narrower than what the question description implies.

**Critical finding:** Despite 4 years of war and over 12,000 documented chemical incidents in Ukraine, **every single confirmed case** involves CS gas (a riot control agent, not on any schedule) or chloropicrin (Schedule 3). No Schedule 1 chemical has been credibly alleged or confirmed in the Ukraine conflict. The EU's May 2025 sanctions, all three OPCW Technical Assistance Visit reports, and NATO/US accusations all specifically reference CS gas and chloropicrin — never nerve agents or other Schedule 1 substances.

Crossing the Schedule 1 threshold would be a qualitatively different escalation that could trigger severe NATO military responses, which explains why Russia has maintained restraint despite extensive lower-level chemical use. The ~2-month window further constrains the probability.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves as Yes if, after the opening of this question and before May 1, 2026, a state actor intentionally uses chemical weapons anywhere within the pre-2014 land territory of Ukraine. The question resolves according to [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) such as accusations from a NATO member state or from the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons ([OPCW](https://www.opcw.org/)).
fine_print:
"State actor" is defined as a government of any [sovereign](https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/sovereignty) state, including its armed forces, security services, or other personnel acting in an official capacity. Only confirmed use of chemical weapons (as defined by the Chemical Weapons Convention) counts. 

"Chemical weapon" means a chemical listed at OPCW's [Annex on Chemicals Schedule 1](https://www.opcw.org/chemical-weapons-convention/annexes/annex-chemicals/schedule-1).
